title: The Solutionist Ethic and the Spirit of Digital Capitalism
author: Nachtwey, Seidl

opening_move.description: The paper opens by noting that existing scholarship on digital capitalism has neglected the normative beliefs of those who design and deploy digital technologies. The gap motivates an empirically grounded account of digital capitalism's cultural underpinnings via the concept of the 'spirit of capitalism' and a new 'solutionist order of worth'.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Understanding the ideology of digital elites is essential for understanding how digital capitalism legitimates, motivates, and orients action — and what limits it faces from its own workers and critics.

theoretical_positioning.description: The paper extends Boltanski and Chiapello's 'new spirit of capitalism' framework by introducing solutionism as a new order of worth specific to digital capitalism, empirically tested via text corpora rather than discursive analysis alone.

epistemic_mode.quantitative_methods: Supervised machine learning (automated nonparametric content analysis, Hopkins and King 2010) applied to three text corpora: public statements of digital elites (n=2,326 paragraphs), Wired magazine articles (c.1.5 million paragraphs, 1993–2019), Harvard Business Review articles (c.190,000 paragraphs, 1980–2022). Hand-coded training set of 1,518 paragraphs with intercoder reliability assessment.
epistemic_mode.qualitative_methods: Illustrative qualitative case analysis of tech elite speeches and statements to interpret findings from the quantitative analysis.
data_sources: Digital elites public statements (Forbes 400 tech founders post-1996)
epistemic_mode.notes: This is the corpus's most empirically intensive paper. The quantitative text analysis is constitutive — without it, the central claim that solutionism is central to digital elite discourse but not yet to capitalist discourse at large could not be made. However, the paper retains a normative-theoretical ambition throughout, using Weber and Boltanski as its interpretive frame.

normative_stake.domain: critical theory of digital capitalism / sociology of ideology

confidence_notes: This paper is an unusual hybrid in the corpus: it combines Weberian-sociology conceptual framework building with quantitative text-as-data methods. The epistemic mode is normative_with_quantitative_empirical because the conceptual claim (solutionism = new order of worth = new spirit of digital capitalism) drives and frames the empirical analysis. However, the empirical evidence is genuinely constitutive — the distributional findings across the three corpora are the paper's main contribution. The secondary mode is primarily_empirical_qualitative because of the illustrative qualitative commentary on tech elite statements. Normative stake is medium: the paper diagnoses ideological structures but does not explicitly call for a political response.
